Dear Medical Director:

I am writing to request prior authorization for a physician-supervised outpatient cardiac rehabilitation program (CPT 93798), 36 sessions over 12 weeks, for the above-referenced 62-year-old female patient.

Clinical Summary:
- Diagnosis: Acute myocardial infarction (ICD-10: I21.4), status post successful percutaneous coronary intervention 3 weeks ago
- Left ventricular ejection fraction: 38% (moderately reduced)
- Functional status: NYHA Class II
- Current status: Hemodynamically stable, medically optimized on guideline-directed medical therapy, highly motivated to participate
- No contraindications to supervised exercise training

Clinical Justification:
The 2013 ACCF/AHA Guideline for the Management of ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction and the 2014 AHA/ACC Guideline for the Management of Patients With Non–ST-Elevation Acute Coronary Syndromes both assign a Class I, Level of Evidence A recommendation for referral to a comprehensive outpatient cardiovascular rehabilitation program for all eligible patients following MI or PCI. This is further supported by the 2019 AHA/ACC/HRS Secondary Prevention Guideline.

Participation in cardiac rehabilitation is associated with a 20–30% reduction in all-cause and cardiovascular mortality, decreased hospital readmissions, and improved functional capacity and quality of life. Given this patient's reduced LVEF (38%), she is at elevated risk for recurrent events and heart failure progression, making structured, monitored exercise and risk-factor modification especially critical.

The standard evidence-based course consists of 36 supervised sessions over 12 weeks, consistent with CMS and commercial payer coverage standards, providing continuous ECG monitoring, blood pressure surveillance, exercise prescription progression, medication titration support, and education on nutrition, smoking cessation, and stress management.

Requ

Dear Medical Director:

I am writing to request prior authorization for a medically necessary, physician-supervised outpatient cardiac rehabilitation program (CPT 93798) for the above-referenced 62-year-old male patient.

Clinical Summary:
The patient is three weeks status-post acute myocardial infarction, successfully treated with percutaneous coronary intervention. His most recent transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrates a reduced left ventricular ejection fraction of 38%, and he is currently NYHA Functional Class II. He is clinically stable on guideline-directed medical therapy, ambulatory, and highly motivated to participate in a structured rehabilitation program. There are no contraindications to supervised exercise training.

Clinical Justification:
Cardiac rehabilitation is a Class I, Level of Evidence A recommendation for all patients following acute myocardial infarction and/or PCI per the 2013 ACCF/AHA Guideline for the Management of ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction and the 2014 AHA/ACC/AATS/PCNA/SCAI/STS Focused Update of the Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Patients With Stable Ischemic Heart Disease. The AHA/ACC Secondary Prevention Guidelines similarly endorse referral. Participation has been shown to reduce all-cause and cardiovascular mortality, decrease hospital readmissions, and improve functional capacity and quality of life.

The requested 36 sessions over 12 weeks is consistent with CMS-covered standards and AACVPR program guidelines for post-MI/post-PCI patients with reduced LVEF.

review.overall_reasoning: The letter provides a clear clinical picture with specific, well-cited guideline support (AHA/ACC Class I, LOE A) and aligns the requested 36 sessions with CMS and AACVPR standards. Dear Medical Director:

I am writing to request prior authorization for semaglutide 2.0 mg subcutaneous injection weekly for my 62-year-old female patient with inadequately controlled type-2 diabetes mellitus and multiple cardiometabolic comorbidities.

Clinical Summary:
- HbA1c: 8.4% (goal <7.0%) despite adherent dual therapy
- Current regimen: metformin 1000 mg BID + SGLT2 inhibitor at maximum tolerated dose
- BMI: 34 kg/m² (Class I obesity)
- Comorbidities: hypertension, dyslipidemia (elevated ASCVD risk)
- No contraindications to GLP-1 receptor agonist therapy (no personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2; no pancreatitis history)

Clinical Justification:
The patient meets evidence-based criteria for GLP-1 receptor agonist intensification. Per the American Diabetes Association Standards of Care in Diabetes—2024 (Section 9: Pharmacologic Approaches to Glycemic Treatment), GLP-1 receptor agonists are preferred agents in patients with type-2 diabetes who have not achieved glycemic targets on existing therapy, particularly those with BMI ≥27 kg/m² and established or high-risk cardiovascular disease. The ADA specifically recommends agents with proven cardiovascular benefit (including semaglutide) independent of HbA1c or metformin use in this population.

Semaglutide additionally addresses this patient's obesity, which contributes directly to her insulin resistance, hypertension, and dyslipidemia. The SUSTAIN-6 trial demonstrated significant reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events with semaglutide in patients with this risk profile.

Without intensification, the patient remains at elevated ris

review.overall_reasoning: The letter provides strong, guideline-anchored justification with specific ADA 2024 section citation, relevant trial evidence (SUSTAIN-6), and documents failure of appropriate prior therapy, BMI, HbA1c, and absence of contraindications. Minor gaps include the letter appearing truncated and lacking explicit duration of prior therapy trials, but the clinical case for approval is compelling.

Dear Clinical Reviewer:

I am writing to request prior authorization for adalimumab 40 mg subcutaneous injection every two weeks (J0135) for the above-referenced 62-year-old female patient with moderate-to-severe, active rheumatoid arthritis (RA).

Clinical Summary:
- Diagnosis: Seropositive RA with persistent synovitis and functional impairment limiting activities of daily living.
- Current disease activity: DAS28 of 5.2, consistent with high disease activity; CRP 34 mg/L, indicating active systemic inflammation.
- Prior therapy: Conventional synthetic DMARD trial with methotrexate titrated to 25 mg weekly for 3 months, with inadequate clinical response and continued elevated inflammatory markers and disease activity scores.
- Contraindications to alternative conventional DMARDs and baseline screening (TB, hepatitis B/C) have been addressed per standard protocol prior to biologic initiation.

Clinical Justification and Guideline Support:
The 2021 American College of Rheumatology (ACR) Guideline for the Treatment of Rheumatoid Arthritis recommends the addition of, or switch to, a biologic DMARD (including TNF inhibitors such as adalimumab) in patients with moderate-to-high disease activity who have had an inadequate response to maximally tolerated methotrexate monotherapy (Fraenkel et al., Arthritis Rheumatol. 2021;73[7]:1108–1123). This patient meets these criteria, having failed an adequate methotrexate trial while demonstrating persistently high disease activity (DAS28 >5.1) and elevated CRP.

Escalation to a TNF inhibitor is clinically indicated to prevent further joint damage, functional decline, and disability. Adalimumab is an evidence-based, first-line biologic option in this setting and is appropriate for

review.overall_reasoning: The letter provides strong, guideline-concordant justification with specific ACR 2021 citation, quantitative disease activity metrics (DAS28 5.2, CRP 34), and documented methotrexate failure at maximally tolerated dose. Minor deductions for truncated ending and lack of explicit TB/hepatitis screening results, but overall a well-constructed request likely to be approved.

Dear Medical Reviewer:

I am writing to request prior authorization for adalimumab 40 mg subcutaneously every two weeks for the above-referenced 62-year-old male patient with moderate-to-severe rheumatoid arthritis (RA) that has failed to respond to conventional synthetic DMARD therapy.

Clinical Summary:
- Diagnosis: Seropositive RA, confirmed by clinical exam and serologic workup.
- Current disease activity: DAS28 of 5.2, consistent with high disease activity.
- Inflammatory markers: CRP 34 mg/L (elevated).
- Functional status: Documented functional impairment limiting activities of daily living.
- Prior therapy: Methotrexate titrated to 25 mg weekly for 3 months with inadequate clinical response and persistent active synovitis. Therapy was tolerated without significant adverse effect, but failed to achieve low disease activity.

Clinical Justification:
Per the American College of Rheumatology 2021 Guideline for the Treatment of Rheumatoid Arthritis, patients with moderate-to-high disease activity despite maximally tolerated methotrexate should be escalated to a biologic DMARD, with TNF inhibitors recommended as an appropriate first-line biologic option. Adalimumab is a well-established TNF inhibitor with strong evidence supporting improved clinical outcomes, radiographic progression, and functional status in this patient population.

Given the patient's objective evidence of persistent high disease activity (DAS28 5.2, CRP 34), functional decline, and documented inadequate response to an adequate trial of methotrexate at therapeutic dosing, he meets step-therapy criteria for advancement to biologic DMARD therapy. Initiation of adalimumab is expected to reduce inflammatory burden, prevent joint damage, and restore function, consistent with evidence-based standards of care.

Requested Authorization:

review.overall_reasoning: The letter provides strong evidence-based justification with specific disease activity metrics (DAS28, CRP), documents an adequate methotrexate trial at therapeutic dose, and cites the ACR 2021 guideline appropriately. Minor gaps include lack of TB/hepatitis screening documentation and vaccination status typically required for TNF inhibitor initiation.

case.condition: MRI brain for new neurologic symptoms
case.guideline: AAN 2024: imaging indicated for new-onset severe headache + focal neurologic exam findings
case.clinical: New-onset severe occipital headache 2 weeks, progressive. Neurologic exam shows mild left-sided hyperreflexia. No prior imaging. Requesting MRI brain with and without contrast. Medicare.

Medicare Prior Authorization Review Board

Re: Prior Authorization Request – MRI Brain With and Without Contrast (CPT 70553)
Patient: [Patient Name]
DOB: [DOB]
Medicare ID: [ID]
Ordering Provider: [Name, NPI]

Dear Clinical Reviewer:

I am writing to request prior authorization for an MRI of the brain with and without contrast (CPT 70553) for the above-referenced 62-year-old female patient. This imaging is medically necessary to evaluate new-onset neurologic symptoms with focal examination findings.

Clinical Summary:
The patient is a 62-year-old woman presenting with a 2-week history of new-onset, severe occipital headache that has been progressive in nature. She has no prior history of chronic headaches or migraines. On neurologic examination, she demonstrates mild left-sided hyperreflexia, a focal finding concerning for an underlying intracranial process. She has had no prior neuroimaging.

The combination of (1) new-onset severe headache in a patient over age 50, (2) progressive symptom course, and (3) focal neurologic exam findings constitutes multiple "red flag" features that warrant urgent neuroimaging to exclude serious intracranial pathology, including but not limited to neoplasm, subacute hemorrhage, vascular malformation, demyelinating disease, or infection. MRI with and without contrast is the preferred modality due to its superior sensitivity for parenchymal, vascular, and neoplastic pathology compared with non-contrast CT.

Guideline Support:
The American Academy of Neurology (AAN) 2024 guidance supports neuroimaging for patients presenting with new-onset severe headache accompanied by focal neurologic examination findings. This patient meets both criteria. The ACR Appropriateness Criteria similarly rate MRI brain with and without contrast as "usually appropriate" (highest category) for headache with associated neurologic deficit.

Requested Service:
- CPT 70553 – MRI brain, without and with contrast
- ICD-10: R51.9 (headache), R29.2 (abnormal reflex)

Delay in authorization could result in progression of an undiagnosed intracranial process. I respectfully request expedited review and approval.

Please contact our office at [phone

review.overall_reasoning: The letter clearly establishes medical necessity by linking specific red-flag features (age >50, new severe progressive headache, focal hyperreflexia) to the cited AAN 2024 criteria and ACR Appropriateness Criteria, with appropriate CPT/ICD-10 coding and professional tone. Minor gaps include absence of vital signs, medication history, and prior workup details, but the request strongly aligns with guideline-supported indications.

Dear Clinical Reviewer:

I am writing to request prior authorization for an MRI of the brain with and without contrast (CPT 70553) for the above-referenced 62-year-old male patient. This study is clinically indicated to evaluate new-onset neurologic symptoms with concerning focal findings.

Clinical Summary:
The patient is a 62-year-old man who presents with a 2-week history of new-onset severe occipital headache that has been progressive in nature. He has no prior history of similar headaches and no prior neuroimaging of the brain. On neurologic examination, he demonstrates mild left-sided hyperreflexia, a focal finding that raises concern for an underlying structural intracranial process.

Clinical Justification:
The combination of (1) new-onset severe headache in a patient over age 50, (2) progressive symptom trajectory over two weeks, and (3) an objective focal neurologic exam finding (left-sided hyperreflexia) constitutes multiple "red flag" features warranting urgent neuroimaging. Differential considerations include intracranial mass lesion, demyelinating disease, cerebrovascular pathology, and other structural etiologies that cannot be excluded on clinical grounds alone. MRI with and without contrast is the preferred modality, as it provides superior soft-tissue resolution and allows characterization of enhancing lesions, which is essential for accurate diagnosis and treatment planning.

Guideline Support:
Per the American Academy of Neurology (AAN) 2024 guidance, neuroimaging is indicated in patients presenting with new-onset severe headache accompanied by focal neurologic exam findings. This patient meets both criteria. MRI is preferred over CT for non-emergent evaluation of headache with focal findings due to its superior diagnostic yield.

Given the progressive nature of symptoms and the presence of a focal neurologic abnormality, timely imaging is essential to exclude serious intracranial pathology and guide appropriate management.

I respectfully request expedited authorization of C

Minor gaps include lack of details on prior conservative management, vital signs, and the letter appears truncated at the end.

Dear Clinical Review Board:

I am writing to request prior authorization for a Dexcom G7 continuous glucose monitor (CGM) and related supplies (A9276–A9278) for my 62-year-old patient with insulin-dependent type 2 diabetes mellitus. This request is medically necessary based on her clinical presentation and current standard-of-care guidelines.

Clinical Summary:
- Diagnosis: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, insulin-requiring
- Current regimen: Multiple daily injections (MDI) — basal-bolus therapy with insulin detemir and insulin aspart
- Most recent HbA1c: 7.8% (above individualized target)
- Hypoglycemia burden: Three or more documented hypoglycemic episodes per month, including a nocturnal hypoglycemic event within the past week
- Self-monitoring of blood glucose (SMBG) via fingerstick has been insufficient to detect asymptomatic and nocturnal hypoglycemia or to guide safe insulin titration

Clinical Justification:
Per the American Diabetes Association Standards of Care in Diabetes—2024 (Section 7: Diabetes Technology), real-time CGM is recommended as standard of care for adults with diabetes on multiple daily injections or continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion, and is specifically endorsed to reduce HbA1c and minimize hypoglycemia (Recommendations 7.13–7.15, Evidence Level A). CMS coverage criteria (effective April 2023) similarly support CGM for beneficiaries on insulin therapy or with a history of problematic hypoglycemia — both of which this patient meets.

This patient's combination of MDI therapy, suboptimal glycemic control, and recurrent hypoglycemia — including a recent nocturnal episode posing significant safety risk — establishes clear med

review.overall_reasoning: The letter provides strong clinical justification with specific ADA 2024 recommendations (7.13-7.15, Evidence Level A) and CMS criteria, clearly documenting MDI therapy, elevated A1c, and recurrent hypoglycemia including a nocturnal event. It appears truncated mid-sentence, but the included content meets medical necessity criteria convincingly.

HCPCS Codes: A9276, A9277, A9278
Diagnosis: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, insulin-treated (ICD-10: E11.65)

Dear Medical Director:

I am writing to request prior authorization for a Dexcom G7 continuous glucose monitor (CGM) and associated supplies for my patient, a 62-year-old man with insulin-dependent type 2 diabetes mellitus. This request is medically necessary based on the clinical circumstances detailed below.

Clinical Summary:
The patient is currently managed on a multiple daily injection (MDI) basal-bolus insulin regimen consisting of insulin detemir and insulin aspart. His most recent HbA1c is 7.8%, reflecting inadequate glycemic control despite intensive insulin therapy. Of greater clinical concern, he experiences documented hypoglycemic events at a frequency of three or more per month, including a nocturnal hypoglycemic episode within the past week. Nocturnal hypoglycemia in an older adult significantly increases the risk of seizure, cardiac arrhythmia, injury, and mortality.

Clinical Justification:
The American Diabetes Association Standards of Care in Diabetes—2024 (Section 7: Diabetes Technology) establishes real-time CGM as the standard of care for adults with diabetes on multiple daily injections of insulin, regardless of type 1 or type 2 classification. ADA recommendations 7.12 and 7.15 specifically endorse CGM to reduce HbA1c and minimize hypoglycemia in MDI-treated patients. CMS Local Coverage Determination criteria for therapeutic CGM (L33822) are also met: the patient has diabetes, is treated with an intensive insulin regimen requiring frequent dose adjustments, and requires frequent blood glucose monitoring.

CGM is expected to (1) detect and alert to nocturnal and asymptomatic hypoglycemia, (2) enable data-driven insulin titration to improve time-in-range, and (3) reduce the risk of severe hypoglycemic events

review.overall_reasoning: The letter presents a strong, well-organized clinical case with specific ADA 2024 recommendation numbers and CMS LCD criteria, clearly documenting MDI insulin use, A1c, and recurrent/nocturnal hypoglycemia. Minor gaps include truncation of the closing and absence of prior SMBG frequency documentation, but the request aligns squarely with guideline-based criteria and is highly likely to be approved.
